stimulus:
While researching a topic, a student has taken the following notes:

  • Iconographic analysis and structural analysis are two approaches to film criticism.
  • Structural analysis examines how a film’s narrative components are arranged across the work.
  • Such an analysis of Akira Kurosawa’s Rashomon might consider how its layered flashback framework destabilizes the viewer’s sense of truth.
  • Iconographic analysis considers the recurring visual symbols a film uses and their cultural meanings.
  • Such an analysis of Wong Kar-wai’s In the Mood for Love might consider how the recurring image of clocks symbolizes the characters’ entrapment in time.
stem: The student wants to present iconographic analysis to an audience unfamiliar with the concept. Which choice most effectively uses relevant information from the notes to accomplish this goal?

answerOptions.content: A structural analysis of Rashomon might consider how its layered flashback framework destabilizes the viewer’s sense of truth.
An approach to film criticism, iconographic analysis considers the recurring visual symbols a film uses and their cultural meanings.
Structural analysis differs from iconographic analysis in that structural analysis examines how a film’s narrative components are arranged across the work.
The recurring image of clocks in In the Mood for Love symbolizes the characters’ entrapment in time.

rationale:
Choice B is the best answer. It defines iconographic analysis directly for an unfamiliar audience, identifying it as an approach to film criticism that considers recurring visual symbols and their meanings.

Choice A gives an example of structural analysis. Choice C contrasts the two methods rather than presenting iconographic analysis on its own terms. Choice D offers an example of iconographic analysis without defining the concept.
